source_metadata.title_raw: Andrew Morris, Esq. - A-18-078 - May 15, 2018 - Truckee

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Andrew Morris, Esq. 
Truckee Town Attorney 
10183 Truckee Airport Road 
Truckee, CA 9616 I 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-078 
Dear Mr. Morris: 
May 15, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act ("Act") 1 on behalf of Town of Truckee Councilmember David Tinnan. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and 
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or 
Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does Councilmember Tinnan have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act in 
taking part in preliminary discussions regarding the Town's participation in the Statewide 
Community Infrastructure Program ("SCIP")? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the discussions will have a material effect on JMA. 
FACTS 
Councilmernber Tinnan is employed by the investment and development finn, JMA 
Ventures, LLC. Among other things, Councilmember Tinnan assists JMA Ventures in obtaining 
land use entitlements from local governments. In this capacity, Councilmember Tinnan has been 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Poliiical Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 througti 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-078 
Page No. 2 
working on a project located within Truckee for which JMA Ventures has obtained certain 
discretionary entitlements. 
Truckee is considering whether to participate in a financing program known as the SCIP. 
SCIP is administered by the California Statewide Communities Development Authority, a joint 
powers authority. SCIP acts as the issuer of bonds, the proceeds of which can be used to pay for 
infrastructure and allow private developers to pay impact fees imposed by local governments. By 
providing for payment of impact fees, with repayment over time, SCIP financing facilitates 
development projects. It benefits developers by offering cheaper financing than may be available 
from commercial lenders and by allowing repayment obligations to be passed on to the ultimate 
buyers of property within development projects. 
The Town Council will decide whether the Town will participate in the SCIP program at all, 
and if so, under what conditions. Town staff anticipate that the initial Council discussion of the 
SCIP program will address only the issues of whether the Town will participate and if so, under 
what conditions. Staff anticipates that any decision about specific projects may occur later, although 
it may not be possible to entirely separate discussion of specific projects from the discussion about 
criteria the Town might use to determine program participation. 
Three developers who have received land use approvals and entitlements for three different 
projects in Truckee have approached the Town and requested that the Town agree to participate in 
the program such that SCIP financing would be available for their projects. One of these developers 
is CouncilmemberTirman's employer, JMA Ventures, which would like to take advantage ofSCIP 
financing for a project upon which Councilmember Tirman has been engaged. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. 
Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Most pertinent 
to the analysis is the following: 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
Foreseeability. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability 
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Interests are 
explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a government decision. 


• 
File No. A-18-078 
Page No. 3 
(Regulation 18701(a).) A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed reasonably 
foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision. If an interest is not explicitly 
involved in the decision, a financial effect is foreseeable if the "effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Considering that JMA is one of three developers that have approached the Town in support 
of participating in the SCIP program to potentially finance an existing project, JMA is likely 
explicitly involved in SCIP-related decisions. However, it is unnecessary to determine if JMA is 
explicitly involved in the decisions, as we conclude below that there is a reasonably foreseeable 
material effect even under the materiality standard for interests not explicitly involved in the 
decision. 
Materiality Standard - Financial Interest in a Source of Income. 
Regulation 18702. l(b) provides the materiality standard for an interest in a business entity, 
including a source of income, not explicitly involved in the decision. (See Regulation 18702.3.) 
Under Regulation 18702.1 (b ), a financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient 
information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock or the value of a 
privately-held business entity, such as decisions that: 
"(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate, or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which 
the business entity is engaged; 
"(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business 
entity." 
JMA approached the Town Counsel and advocated for the Town to adopt the SCIP program 
in the hope that SCIP funding would be available to JMA for the project. In this way, the Town 
Council's decision to participate in the SCIP would have the effect of establishing conditions for 
JMA's development project. Moreover, the decision's effect would directly increase or decrease the 
financial liability of JMA. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable that the discussions will have a 
material effect on JMA. 
In addition, Regulation 18702.3( c) sets forth the nexus test, an alternative materiality 
standard potentially applicable to an official's source-of-income interest. Under this test, "[ a ]ny 
reasonably foreseeable effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed 
material if the public official receives or is promised income to achieve a goal or purpose which 
would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision." The rationale for the nexus test is 
that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or 
she does as an official, we presume that the private employer is benefiting from the actions of the 
employee in his or her official capacity. (See Torres Advice Letter, No. A-17-117.) In this case, 
Councilmember Tinnan receives income to assist JMA in obtaining land use entitlements from 
local governments. A goal that would be aided or hindered by decisions relating to the SCIP 
program. Accordingly, we also find a reasonably foreseeable material effect on JMA based upon 
the nexus test. 


File No. A-18-078 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
7ro~ 
By: 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division

taking part in preliminary discussions regarding the Town's participation in the Statewide 
Community Infrastructure Program ("SCIP")?

CONCLUSION: Yes. It is reasonably foreseeable that the discussions will have a material effect on JMA.
